stimulus: In the late nineteenth century, some settler-descendant ranchers on the southern Great Plains adopted range-management methods that the Comanche people had developed many decades earlier, but it appears that few of those ranchers had ever spent time on Comanche-managed land themselves. Setting aside the unlikely possibility that several ranchers of the period independently invented techniques that the Comanche people had already created, these facts most strongly suggest that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: Comanche range-management methods were also widely employed in regions well outside the southern Great Plains.
those ranchers acquired the techniques from other people who had been more directly influenced by Comanche practices.
settler-descendant ranchers in the region only began appreciating the value of Comanche range-management methods in the early twentieth century.
the kinds of livestock typically raised by settler-descendant ranchers on the southern Great Plains were poorly suited to Comanche range-management methods.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer because it most logically completes the discussion. The text presents two facts—the ranchers used Comanche techniques, and the ranchers did not get them firsthand from Comanche-managed land—and rules out coincidence. The remaining plausible explanation is that the techniques reached the ranchers through intermediaries: people who had more direct contact with Comanche practices and passed the techniques along.

Choice A is incorrect because the text discusses use only on the southern Great Plains and gives no evidence about other regions. Choice C is incorrect because the text says the ranchers were already using the techniques in the late nineteenth century, contradicting a claim that they only began appreciating them in the early twentieth. Choice D is incorrect because the text says the ranchers actually adopted and used the techniques—so the techniques cannot have been unsuited to their livestock.
